final_answer: TruthfulQA addresses the problem of measuring how well language models produce truthful answers to questions, specifically focusing on their tendency to mimic human falsehoods and popular misconceptions. Its main finding is that baseline language models, including GPT-3 and others, have low truthfulness in zero-shot settings, with the best model truthful on only 58% of questions compared to 94% for humans. Additionally, larger models tend to be less truthful, often generating false but informative answers that can be more deceptive.

Existing datasets for LLM hallucina- tion detection are predominantly synthesized (Li et al., 2023). For instance, in Liu and Liu (2023); Longpre et al. While these approaches are efficient at generating hallucinations, the re- sulting artificial hallucinations can substantially differ from those that naturally occur. In Chen et al. (2023); Hu et al. final_answer: RAGTruth provides a large-scale, high-quality dataset specifically designed for word-level hallucination detection in Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) scenarios. It consists of nearly 18,000 fully annotated natural responses generated from major open-source and closed-source LLMs, covering several common tasks such as question answering, data-to-text writing, and news summarization. The annotation granularity is at the word-level, including special span-level annotations like due_to_null for certain hallucination types. The paper claims that by fine-tuning a relatively small LLM on the RAGTruth dataset, it is possible to achieve competitive hallucination detection performance comparable to prompt-based approaches using state-of-the-art LLMs like GPT-4, and that the fine-tuned model can effectively mitigate hallucination in LLM responses.

final_answer: The main problem addressed by the paper is the "mirage effect" in current AI models, particularly those with joint image-text training. This effect causes models to generate descriptions and base their reasoning on multi-modal inputs, such as images, that were never actually provided, thereby creating an illusion of visual understanding. This behavior can lead to significant real-world consequences, such as confidently claiming the presence of objects in absent images or suggesting medical diagnoses without actual visual input.The central finding is that frontier models exhibit high rates of this mirage behavior. Specifically, models can answer visual questions in the absence of images with high accuracy, often retaining 70-80% of their fully image-enabled accuracies (and up to 99% in some individual benchmarks, especially medical ones). This suggests that the models' performance on visual benchmarks is largely driven by non-visual (textual) inference rather than genuine grounding in visual input.

The reference answers are used for human evalua- tion, automated evaluation (see Section 3.2), and a multiple-choice task (Section 3.1). Their construc- tion is described in Appendix C.1. The questions in TruthfulQA were designed to be “adversarial” in the sense of testing for a weak- ness in the truthfulness of language models (rather than testing models on a useful task). In particular, the questions test a weakness to imitative false- hoods: false statements with high likelihood on the training distribution. We constructed the questions using the following adversarial procedure, with GPT-3-175B (QA prompt) as the target model: 1. We wrote questions that some humans would answer falsely. We tested them on the tar- get model and ﬁltered out questions that the model consistently answered correctly when multiple random samples were generated at nonzero temperatures. We produced 437 ques- tions this way, which we call the “ﬁltered” questions (Wallace and Boyd-Graber, 2018). 2. Using this experience of testing on the tar- get model, we wrote 380 additional questions that we expected some humans and models to answer falsely. Since we did not test on the target model, these are “unﬁltered” questions. We report results on the combined ﬁltered and unﬁltered questions. For non-combined results, see Appendix B.4. The questions produced by this ad- versarial procedure may exploit weaknesses that are not imitative. For example, the target model might answer a question falsely
final_answer: The authors created 437 filtered questions and 380 unfiltered questions when constructing TruthfulQA.

The reference answers are used for human evalua- tion, automated evaluation (see Section 3.2), and a multiple-choice task (Section 3.1). Their construc- tion is described in Appendix C.1. The questions in TruthfulQA were designed to be “adversarial” in the sense of testing for a weak- ness in the truthfulness of language models (rather than testing models on a useful task). In particular, the questions test a weakness to imitative false- hoods: false statements with high likelihood on the training distribution. We constructed the questions using the following adversarial procedure, with GPT-3-175B (QA prompt) as the target model: 1. We wrote questions that some humans would answer falsely. We tested them on the tar- get model and ﬁltered out questions that the model consistently answered correctly when multiple random samples were generated at nonzero temperatures. We produced 437 ques- tions this way, which we call the “ﬁltered” questions (Wallace and Boyd-Graber, 2018). 2. Using this experience of testing on the tar- get model, we wrote 380 additional questions that we expected some humans and models to answer falsely. Since we did not test on the target model, these are “unﬁltered” questions. We report results on the combined ﬁltered and unﬁltered questions. For non-combined results, see Appendix B.4. paper_003_chunk_0004 | Abstract | score=5.392837 | et al., 2023). However, uncertainty metrics such as token probability or entropy require access to token-level probability distributions, information which may not be available to users for example when systems are accessed through limited exter- nal APIs. An alternate approach is to leverage fact-verification approaches, where evidence is re- trieved from an external database to assess the ve- racity of a claim (Thorne et al., 2018; Guo et al., 2022). However, facts can only be assessed relative to the knowledge present in the database. Addition- 1 arXiv:2303.08896v3 [cs.CL] 11 Oct 2023 ally, hallucinations are observed over a wide range of tasks beyond pure fact verification (Kryscinski et al., 2020; Maynez et al., 2020). In this paper, we propose SelfCheckGPT, a sampling-based approach that can detect whether responses generated by LLMs are hallucinated or factual. To the best of our knowledge, SelfCheck- GPT is the first work to analyze model halluci- nation of general LLM responses, and is the first zero-resource hallucination detection solution that can be applied to black-box systems. The motivat- ing idea of SelfCheckGPT is that when an LLM has been trained on a given concept, the sampled re- sponses are likely to be similar and contain consis- tent facts. However, for hallucinated facts, stochas- tically sampled responses are likely to diverge and may contradict one another. By sampling multiple responses from an LLM, one can measure informa- tion consistency between the different responses
final_answer: SelfCheckGPT addresses the problem of detecting whether responses generated by Large Language Models (LLMs) are hallucinated or factual. It provides a zero-resource hallucination detection solution that can be applied to black-box systems, overcoming limitations of methods that require access to token-level probability distributions or external fact databases.The central idea behind SelfCheckGPT's method is that when an LLM has been trained on a given concept, stochastically sampled responses are likely to be similar and contain consistent facts. In contrast, for hallucinated facts, stochastically sampled responses are likely to diverge and may contradict one another. By sampling multiple responses from an LLM, SelfCheckGPT measures the information consistency between these different responses to identify hallucinations.

final_answer: HaluEval addresses the problem of understanding the types and extent to which Large Language Models (LLMs) tend to hallucinate, and it challenges their ability to recognize hallucinations and analyze their information blind spots. Its main empirical findings about LLM hallucination recognition are:1.  ChatGPT is prone to generating hallucinated content by fabricating unverifiable information, with approximately 19.5% of its responses containing such fabrications across topics like language, climate, and technology.2.  Existing LLMs, including ChatGPT itself (which generated the hallucinated samples), face significant challenges in identifying hallucinations in generated text. For instance, ChatGPT achieved only 62.59% accuracy in question answering tasks requiring hallucination recognition.3.  The performance of LLMs in recognizing hallucinations can be improved by providing explicit knowledge and incorporating intermediate reasoning steps. However, contrasting hallucinated samples directly with ground-truth information can confuse LLMs and lead to worse performance.

Each instance contains responses from 6 different models, totaling nearly 18,000 fully annotated natural responses. final_answer: Based on the returned evidence, the paper investigates whether multimodal/vision-language models genuinely understand images or merely produce plausible-sounding answers using non-visual priors (e.g., language patterns, benchmark structure, or memorized/leaked data). The central finding is that when tested without any image input, models (e.g., Gemini, GPT-4.1, Claude Sonnet) still generate confident, detailed, and seemingly visually-grounded answers—often fabricating specific visual details (e.g., different models 'seeing' Saturn or the Moon for the same Mars image prompt, or different languages for the same OCR prompt)—and this no-image 'mirage-mode' performance is comparable to or even better than an explicit 'guessing-mode' across medical benchmarks and most MMMU-Pro categories. This indicates that current multimodal benchmarks are compromised by data leakage and hidden benchmark-level patterns, so high scores reflect exploitation of such artifacts rather than true visual understanding, and the paper argues that simply creating new benchmarks is only a temporary fix since they too become absorbed into pretraining data.

final_answer: TruthfulQA addresses the problem of measuring whether language models generate truthful answers, specifically testing whether models are prone to generating false answers that mimic human falsehoods and misconceptions rather than being merely unable to answer correctly. It is a benchmark of 817 questions spanning 38 categories, designed to be answered in a zero-shot setting. The main finding is that baseline language models have low truthfulness: the best-performing model tested (GPT-3-175B with a 'helpful' prompt) was truthful on only 58% of questions under human evaluation, compared to 94% for human performance, and this same model produced answers that were both false and informative 42% of the time (versus 6% for humans), with such informative-but-false answers often mimicking popular misconceptions and being more likely to deceive. Additionally, the paper finds that larger models tend to be less truthful than smaller ones on this benchmark, in contrast to typical trends on other NLP tasks.

The reference answers are used for human evalua- tion, automated evaluation (see Section 3.2), and a multiple-choice task (Section 3.1). Their construc- tion is described in Appendix C.1. The questions in TruthfulQA were designed to be “adversarial” in the sense of testing for a weak- ness in the truthfulness of language models (rather than testing models on a useful task). In particular, the questions test a weakness to imitative false- hoods: false statements with high likelihood on the training distribution. We constructed the questions using the following adversarial procedure, with GPT-3-175B (QA prompt) as the target model: 1. We wrote questions that some humans would answer falsely. We tested them on the tar- get model and ﬁltered out questions that the model consistently answered correctly when multiple random samples were generated at nonzero temperatures. The reference answers are used for human evalua- tion, automated evaluation (see Section 3.2), and a multiple-choice task (Section 3.1). Their construc- tion is described in Appendix C.1. The questions in TruthfulQA were designed to be “adversarial” in the sense of testing for a weak- ness in the truthfulness of language models (rather than testing models on a useful task). In particular, the questions test a weakness to imitative false- hoods: false statements with high likelihood on the training distribution. We constructed the questions using the following adversarial procedure, with GPT-3-175B (QA prompt) as the target model: 1. We wrote questions that some humans would answer falsely. We tested them on the tar- get model and ﬁltered out questions that the model consistently answered correctly when multiple random samples were generated at nonzero temperatures. We produced 437 ques- tions this way, which we call the “ﬁltered” questions (Wallace and Boyd-Graber, 2018). 2. Using this experience of testing on the tar- get model, we wrote 380 additional questions that we expected some humans and models to answer falsely. Since we did not test on the target model, these are “unﬁltered” questions. We report results on the combined ﬁltered and unﬁltered questions. For non-combined results, see Appendix B.4. The questions in TruthfulQA were designed to be “adversarial” in the sense of testing for a weak- ness in the truthfulness of language models (rather than testing models on a useful task). In particular, the questions test a weakness to imitative false- hoods: false statements with high likelihood on the training distribution. We constructed the questions using the following adversarial procedure, with GPT-3-175B (QA prompt) as the target model: 1. We wrote questions that some humans would answer falsely. final_answer: SelfCheckGPT addresses the problem of detecting hallucinations in responses generated by large language models—i.e., determining whether facts/statements produced by an LLM are factual or fabricated—in a zero-resource, black-box setting where internal token-level probabilities are unavailable (e.g., due to limited API access) and no external database exists to verify claims. The central idea behind its method is sampling-based self-consistency checking: since an LLM's responses about a concept it truly 'knows' tend to be similar and mutually consistent when sampled multiple times, while hallucinated facts tend to diverge or contradict each other across stochastic samples, one can measure the informational consistency among multiple sampled responses to the same prompt to determine whether a given statement is likely factual or hallucinated. This requires only sampled outputs from the model (no external knowledge source or internal probabilities), making it applicable to black-box LLMs. The paper proposes five variants of this consistency measurement: BERTScore, question-answering, n-gram, NLI, and LLM prompting.

final_answer: HaluEval addresses the problem of evaluating and understanding hallucination in large language models (LLMs) by providing a large-scale benchmark of correct samples paired with hallucinated counterparts (generated via a sampling-then-filtering pipeline plus human annotation) across tasks such as question answering, dialogue, and summarization. The benchmark is designed to test LLMs' ability to recognize hallucinated content and to probe LLMs' 'information blind spots.' Empirically, the paper finds: (1) ChatGPT itself is prone to generating hallucinated content, fabricating unverifiable information in about 19.5% of its responses, on topics spanning language, climate, and technology; (2) existing LLMs face significant challenges in identifying hallucinations in generated text—even ChatGPT, which was used to generate the hallucinated samples, only achieves about 62.59% accuracy in question answering, and in text summarization ChatGPT reaches only 58.53% accuracy (barely above chance), while GPT-3 is around 50% (chance level) across three tasks, with Alpaca and Vicuna performing even worse (below random chance); and (3) this deficient recognition performance can be improved by providing explicit knowledge and adding intermediate reasoning steps, whereas contrasting hallucinated samples directly with ground-truth answers tends to confuse LLMs further and worsens performance.
